message.reasoning_content:
We are to assess the criteria based on the title and abstract. Let's analyze each criterion.

First, understand the document: It's a review ("This review explores...") of prompt engineering for LLMs. It mentions specific techniques like self-consistency, chain of thought, generated knowledge. It also discusses prompt methods for VLMs, evaluation metrics, AI security, and future perspectives.

Now evaluate each criterion.

Criterion I1_PROMPT_TECHNIQUE: "The article describes or proposes at least one concrete prompt-engineering operation: a way to construct, structure, sequence, transform, verify, or constrain prompts or model outputs. Merely using an LLM, evaluating an LLM, or naming a prompting family does not by itself satisfy this criterion."

The abstract mentions "structuring inputs" (S2) and lists techniques like self-consistency, chain of thought, generated knowledge (S3). These are concrete prompt engineering operations. Also mentions "prompt methods" for VLMs (S4) and evaluation with metrics (S5). So it describes multiple concrete techniques. Status: met. Evidence: S2: "Prompt engineering is the process of structuring inputs", S3: "techniques such as self-consistency, chain of thought, and generated knowledge".

Criterion I2_REPRODUCIBLE: "The title or abstract exposes an identifiable prompt structure, instruction, sequence, procedure, or transferable method that a reader could reproduce or operationalize. Reporting only comparative performance of named prompting methods, without describing a transferable technique or structure, does not satisfy this criterion."

The abstract names specific techniques (chain of thought, self-consistency, generated knowledge) which are identifiable and transferable methods. A reader could reproduce these. The abstract also mentions "structuring inputs" as process. So it does expose identifiable methods. Status: met. Evidence: S3 lists the techniques.

Criterion I3_LLM_TARGET: "The technique targets large language models or agents whose behavior is based on large language models."

The abstract explicitly mentions "large language models (LLMs)" (S1), "prompt engineering for LLMs" (title), and also "vision language models (VLMs)" (S4). So it targets LLMs. Status: met. Evidence: title, S1.

Criterion I4_PRACTITIONER: "The technique is applicable by a practitioner through prompts, instructions, context, inference-time orchestration, or output constraints, without requiring the practitioner to train or fine-tune model weights or learned prompt parameters."

The techniques described (self-consistency, chain of thought, generated knowledge) are all prompt-based methods that do not require training or fine-tuning. They are applied at inference time. So status: met. Evidence: S3 lists them.

Now exclusion criteria.

E1_NO_ACTIONABLE_TECHNIQUE: "The article discusses LLMs or agents but does not propose or describe an actionable prompt technique."

Since we already found it describes actionable techniques (I1 met), this is false. So status: not_met. Evidence: S2 and S3 show description of actionable techniques.

E2_MODEL_TRAINING_ONLY: "The article is solely about model architecture, training, fine-tuning, model weights, or learned prompt parameters, without a practitioner-usable prompting component."

This article is about prompt engineering, not about model training. So status: not_met. Evidence: Title and S1 focus on prompt engineering.

E3_BENCHMARK_ONLY: "The article is a benchmark or model evaluation without a transferable prompt method, prompt structure, or prompting procedure."

It is a review, not a benchmark. It describes methods, not just evaluation. So status: not_met. Evidence: S2, S3 describe methods.

E4_APPLICATION_WITHOUT_PROMPT_DETAIL: "The article reports a domain application of an LLM without describing the prompt method in actionable detail."

It is a review, not a domain application. It describes prompt methods in general. So status: not_met. Evidence: Title says "review", S1 says "explores the role".

E5_NON_PRIMARY_WITHOUT_ISOLABLE_TECHNIQUE: "The article is an opinion, editorial, or survey that does not expose at least one isolable and actionable prompt technique. A survey can avoid this exclusion when its title or abstract actually identifies transferable techniques or structures."

It is a survey/review, but it does identify transferable techniques (self-consistency, etc.). So it avoids exclusion. Status: not_met. Evidence: S3 identifies techniques.

Now produce JSON. For each, provide evidence and reason.
